description:
Your complete GATE 2027 CS/IT roadmap.

📅 Timeline: May 11, 2026 → Feb 2027
• 6 months core study (May–Nov)
• 1 month revision (Nov–Dec)
• Mock test phase (Dec–Feb)

📊 GATE CS Syllabus (11 subjects):
1. Engineering Mathematics — ~15 marks
2. Digital Logic — ~4 marks
3. Computer Organization & Architecture — ~6 marks
4. Programming in C + Data Structures — ~12 marks
5. Algorithms — ~6 marks
6. Theory of Computation — ~6 marks
7. Compiler Design — ~4 marks
8. Operating Systems — ~8 marks
9. Computer Networks — ~6 marks
10. Databases — ~6 marks
11. Software Engineering — ~3 marks

General Aptitude: 15 marks (easy — must score 12+)

🎯 Target: 60+ marks to get AIR < 500

⏰ DAILY SCHEDULE (6 hrs/day):
• Morning (2 hrs): Study new topic — read concepts, make notes
• Afternoon (2 hrs): Solve GATE PYQs on today's topic
• Evening (1.5 hrs): Practice problems from standard books
• Night (30 min): Quick revision of today's content

📅 WEEKLY PATTERN:
• Mon–Fri: New topics + PYQs
• Saturday: Weekly subject test (timed)
• Sunday: Rest or catch-up

📚 STANDARD BOOKS:
• Discrete Math: Kenneth Rosen
• Algorithms: CLRS (Cormen)
• OS: Tanenbaum / Silberschatz
• Networks: Tanenbaum / Forouzan
• DBMS: Korth / Navathe
• Compiler: Aho-Lam-Sethi-Ullman (Dragon Book)
• TOC: Sipser / Ullman

🌐 ONLINE RESOURCES:
• gateoverflow.in — best PYQ explanations
• Virtual GATE on official GATE portal
• Made Easy / ACE Academy notes

⚠️ KEY HABITS:
1. Solve min 10 PYQs per subtopic
2. Maintain a formula/concept sheet per subject
3. Never skip Engg Math — 15 marks!
4. Revision > new study in last 2 months
5. Attempt all General Aptitude questions (no excuse to miss)

🎯 GATE EXAM FORMAT:
• 65 questions, 100 marks, 3 hours
• 1-mark questions: -1/3 negative marking
• 2-mark questions: -2/3 negative marking
• MCQ + MSQ + NAT (Numerical Answer Type — no negative marking!)

⚡ EXAM DAY STRATEGY:
1. First 15 min: Scan all 65 questions, mark easy ones
2. Attempt all 1-mark questions first
3. Solve confident 2-mark questions
4. NAT questions — always attempt (no negative!)
5. Skip doubtful MCQs (penalty is real)
6. Last 15 min: Review + educated guesses only where >50% sure

❌ AVOID:
• >5 min on any single question
• Blind guessing MCQs
• Skipping General Aptitude

📝 REGISTRATION:
• GATE 2027 registration: typically Sep–Oct 2026
• Portal: goaps.iitkgp.ac.in
• Exam: Feb 2027 (check official notification)
• Application fee: ~₹1800 (general category)

📅 May 11 – June 10, 2026

🎯 Build mathematical foundation. Engineering Math = ~15 marks in GATE.

Topics this month:
• Propositional & Predicate Logic
• Set Theory, Relations, Functions
• Graph Theory (basics)
• Combinatorics & Counting
• Linear Algebra
• Calculus
• Probability & Statistics

Weekly Plan:
• Week 1 (May 11-17): Logic + Set Theory + Relations
• Week 2 (May 18-24): Functions + Graph Theory + Combinatorics
• Week 3 (May 25-31): Linear Algebra
• Week 4 (Jun 1-10): Calculus + Probability + PYQ practice

📅 May 11–17, 2026

Day 1 Mon May 11: Propositional Logic — connectives, truth tables, tautology, contradiction, logical equivalence
Day 2 Tue May 12: Propositional Logic — inference rules (Modus Ponens, Tollens), resolution, CNF/DNF
Day 3 Wed May 13: Predicate Logic — quantifiers (∀, ∃), FOL, negation of quantifiers
Day 4 Thu May 14: Set Theory — operations, power sets, Cartesian product, Venn diagrams
Day 5 Fri May 15: Relations — reflexive, symmetric, transitive, equivalence, partial order, POSET, Hasse diagram
Day 6 Sat May 16: Solve 20 GATE PYQs on Logic + Sets (timed)
Day 7 Sun May 17: Revise week, update formula sheet

PROPOSITIONAL LOGIC:

Connectives: ¬ NOT, ∧ AND, ∨ OR, → Implies, ↔ Biconditional

Key Equivalences:
• De Morgan: ¬(P∧Q) = ¬P∨¬Q and ¬(P∨Q) = ¬P∧¬Q
• Implication: P→Q = ¬P∨Q
• Contrapositive: P→Q ≡ ¬Q→¬P
• Double Negation: ¬¬P = P
• Tautology: P∨¬P always TRUE
• Contradiction: P∧¬P always FALSE

Inference Rules:
• Modus Ponens: P, P→Q ⊢ Q
• Modus Tollens: ¬Q, P→Q ⊢ ¬P
• Hypothetical Syllogism: P→Q, Q→R ⊢ P→R
• Resolution: (P∨Q) ∧ (¬P∨R) ⊢ Q∨R

Normal Forms:
• CNF (Conjunctive Normal Form): AND of ORs — clausal form
• DNF (Disjunctive Normal Form): OR of ANDs

PREDICATE LOGIC:
• ∀x P(x) — true for all x
• ∃x P(x) — true for some x
• ¬∀x P(x) = ∃x ¬P(x)
• ¬∃x P(x) = ∀x ¬P(x)

GATE FOCUS: Is formula tautology/contradiction? Convert to CNF/DNF. Resolution proofs. Negate quantified formulas.

REFERENCES:
📺 Neso Academy (Logic): https://www.youtube.com/@nesoacademy
📺 Search: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=propositional+logic+gate+exam+neso
📝 PYQs: https://gateoverflow.in/tag/propositional-logic
📚 Book: Kenneth Rosen — Discrete Math Ch.1 (Propositional Logic) + Ch.1.4 (Predicates)

SET THEORY:

Operations: Union ∪, Intersection ∩, Difference −, Complement A'
Power Set: |P(A)| = 2^|A|
Inclusion-Exclusion: |A∪B| = |A|+|B|−|A∩B|
|A∪B∪C| = |A|+|B|+|C| − |A∩B| − |B∩C| − |A∩C| + |A∩B∩C|

RELATIONS (on set A):
• Reflexive: (a,a) ∈ R for all a ∈ A
• Symmetric: (a,b)∈R → (b,a)∈R
• Antisymmetric: (a,b)∈R and (b,a)∈R → a=b
• Transitive: (a,b)∈R and (b,c)∈R → (a,c)∈R

Equivalence Relation = Reflexive + Symmetric + Transitive
  → partitions set into equivalence classes

Partial Order (POSET) = Reflexive + Antisymmetric + Transitive
  → Hasse diagram: draw without reflexive/transitive edges
  → Lattice: every pair has LUB and GLB

Closure: Reflexive closure adds (a,a), Transitive closure uses Warshall's algorithm

GATE FOCUS: Identify relation type, count equivalence classes, number of relations with given property on n-element set.

REFERENCES:
📺 Neso Academy (Set Theory): https://www.youtube.com/@nesoacademy
📺 Search: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=set+theory+relations+gate+neso+academy
📝 PYQs: https://gateoverflow.in/tag/set-theory
📚 Book: Kenneth Rosen — Discrete Math Ch.2 (Sets) + Ch.9 (Relations)

📅 May 18–24, 2026

Day 1 Mon May 18: Functions — injective, surjective, bijective, composition, inverse
Day 2 Tue May 19: Pigeonhole Principle, counting functions, Stirling numbers
Day 3 Wed May 20: Graph Theory — basic definitions, degree sequence, handshaking lemma
Day 4 Thu May 21: Graph Theory — paths, cycles, connectivity, Euler/Hamiltonian paths, planar graphs
Day 5 Fri May 22: Combinatorics — permutations, combinations, stars & bars, inclusion-exclusion
Day 6 Sat May 23: Combinatorics — binomial theorem, derangements, recurrences
Day 7 Sun May 24: Solve 20 PYQs + Revise

FUNCTIONS:

Types:
• Injective (one-to-one): f(a)=f(b)→a=b
• Surjective (onto): every codomain element has a preimage
• Bijective: both (invertible)

Counting functions from A(size m) to B(size n):
• Total functions: n^m
• Injective: P(n,m) = n!/(n−m)! if n≥m, else 0
• Surjective: inclusion-exclusion formula
• Bijective (m=n): n!

Pigeonhole Principle:
• n+1 items in n boxes → some box has ≥2 items
• Generalized: kn+1 items in n boxes → some box has ≥k+1 items

COMBINATORICS:
• Permutations P(n,r) = n!/(n−r)!
• Combinations C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n−r)!)
• Multiset (repetition allowed): C(n+r−1, r)
• Stars & Bars: x₁+x₂+…+xₖ=n non-negative solutions = C(n+k−1, k−1)
• Derangement D(n) = n! × Σ(−1)^k/k! ≈ n!/e
• Binomial: (x+y)^n = Σ C(n,k) x^k y^(n−k)

GATE FOCUS: Count injective/surjective/bijective functions, pigeonhole application, combinations with constraints.

REFERENCES:
📺 Neso Academy (Combinatorics): https://www.youtube.com/@nesoacademy
📺 Search: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=counting+combinatorics+gate+cs+pigeonhole
📝 PYQs: https://gateoverflow.in/tag/combinatorics
📚 Book: Kenneth Rosen — Discrete Math Ch.6 (Counting) + Ch.8 (Advanced Counting)

BASIC DEFINITIONS:

Handshaking Lemma: Σ deg(v) = 2|E| (sum of degrees = twice number of edges)
Complete graph Kn: n(n−1)/2 edges, every vertex has degree n−1
Bipartite: vertices split into 2 sets, edges only between sets (no odd cycles)

TRAVERSALS:
• Euler path: visits every EDGE once — exists iff exactly 0 or 2 vertices of odd degree
• Euler circuit: visits every edge, returns to start — all vertices must have even degree
• Hamiltonian path/cycle: visits every VERTEX once — NP-complete to decide

TREES:
• Connected acyclic graph; n vertices → n−1 edges
• Spanning tree: subgraph that is a tree containing all vertices
• Minimum Spanning Tree (MST): spanning tree with minimum total edge weight

PLANARITY:
• Euler's formula for connected planar graph: V − E + F = 2
• For simple planar graph: E ≤ 3V − 6
• K5 and K3,3 are non-planar (Kuratowski's theorem)

GRAPH COLORING:
• Chromatic number χ(G) = min colors to color vertices (no two adjacent same color)
• 4-color theorem: any planar graph is 4-colorable
• Bipartite graphs are 2-colorable (χ = 2)

GATE FOCUS: Is graph Eulerian? Planar graph V−E+F check. Tree properties. Chromatic number.

REFERENCES:
📺 Neso Academy (Graph Theory): https://www.youtube.com/@nesoacademy
📺 Search: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=graph+theory+discrete+math+gate+euler+planar
📝 PYQs: https://gateoverflow.in/tag/graph-theory
📚 Book: Kenneth Rosen — Discrete Math Ch.10 (Graphs) + Ch.11 (Trees)

📅 May 25–31, 2026

Day 1 Sat May 25: Matrices — operations, determinant, inverse, properties
Day 2 Sun May 26: Rank — row echelon form, rank-nullity theorem, linear independence
Day 3 Mon May 27: Systems of linear equations — consistency conditions (Rouché-Capelli theorem)
Day 4 Tue May 28: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors — characteristic equation
Day 5 Wed May 29: Special matrices — symmetric, orthogonal, idempotent, nilpotent; Cayley-Hamilton
Day 6 Thu May 30: Solve 20 GATE PYQs on Linear Algebra
Day 7 Fri May 31: Revise, update formula sheet

MATRIX PROPERTIES:
• det(AB) = det(A)·det(B)
• det(Aᵀ) = det(A)
• det(kA) = kⁿ·det(A) for n×n matrix
• det(A⁻¹) = 1/det(A)
• A⁻¹ exists iff det(A) ≠ 0

RANK:
• rank = number of non-zero rows in row echelon form
• rank(A) + nullity(A) = n (columns)
• rank(AB) ≤ min(rank(A), rank(B))
• rank(A) = rank(Aᵀ) = rank(AᵀA)

SYSTEM Ax = b:
• Consistent iff rank(A) = rank(A|b)
• Unique solution iff rank(A) = rank(A|b) = n
• Infinite solutions iff rank(A) = rank(A|b) < n
• No solution iff rank(A) ≠ rank(A|b)

EIGENVALUES:
• Find: det(A − λI) = 0 (characteristic equation)
• Trace(A) = Σλᵢ (sum of eigenvalues)
• det(A) = Πλᵢ (product of eigenvalues)
• Cayley-Hamilton: A satisfies its own characteristic equation
• A and Aᵀ have same eigenvalues

SPECIAL MATRICES:
• Symmetric (A=Aᵀ): real eigenvalues, orthogonal eigenvectors
• Orthogonal (AᵀA=I): eigenvalues ±1, det = ±1
• Idempotent (A²=A): eigenvalues are 0 or 1
• Nilpotent (Aᵏ=0 for some k): all eigenvalues are 0

GATE FOCUS: Rank of matrix, eigenvalue sum/product, system consistency, Cayley-Hamilton application.

REFERENCES:
📺 3Blue1Brown (visual intuition): https://www.youtube.com/@3blue1brown
📺 Search: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=linear+algebra+eigenvalues+gate+nptel
📝 PYQs: https://gateoverflow.in/tag/linear-algebra
📚 Book: Gilbert Strang — Introduction to Linear Algebra Ch.1-6
📚 NPTEL: https://nptel.ac.in (search "Linear Algebra" IIT course)

📅 June 1–10, 2026

Day 1 Mon Jun 1: Calculus — limits, continuity, L'Hôpital's rule
Day 2 Tue Jun 2: Derivatives — chain rule, MVT, maxima/minima
Day 3 Wed Jun 3: Integration — definite integrals, integration by parts
Day 4 Thu Jun 4: Probability — sample space, events, conditional probability
Day 5 Fri Jun 5: Bayes' theorem, random variables, distributions
Day 6 Sat Jun 6: Statistics — mean, variance, common distributions (Binomial, Poisson, Normal)
Day 7-9 Jun 7-9: Solve 30 GATE PYQs across all Month 1 topics
Day 10 Jun 10: Full Month 1 revision test (timed, 30 questions)

LIMITS & CONTINUITY:
• L'Hôpital's Rule: for 0/0 or ∞/∞ form, lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x)
• Continuity: lim(x→a) f(x) = f(a)

DERIVATIVES:
• Power: d/dx(xⁿ) = nxⁿ⁻¹
• Chain: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x))·g'(x)
• Product: (uv)' = u'v + uv'
• Quotient: (u/v)' = (u'v − uv')/v²
• eˣ: d/dx(eˣ) = eˣ; ln x: d/dx(ln x) = 1/x

Mean Value Theorem: ∃c ∈ (a,b): f'(c) = [f(b)−f(a)]/(b−a)
Rolle's Theorem: f(a)=f(b) → ∃c: f'(c)=0

INTEGRATION:
• ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C
• ∫eˣ dx = eˣ + C
• ∫(1/x) dx = ln|x| + C
• By parts: ∫u dv = uv − ∫v du (LIATE rule for choosing u)

MAXIMA/MINIMA:
• Critical points: f'(x) = 0
• f''(x) > 0 → local min; f''(x) < 0 → local max

GATE FOCUS: Usually 1-2 simple questions on limits (L'Hôpital), derivatives, or definite integrals.

REFERENCES:
📺 Khan Academy (Calculus): https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=khan+academy+calculus+limits+derivatives
📺 Search: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=calculus+gate+engineering+mathematics+limits
📝 PYQs: https://gateoverflow.in/tag/calculus
📚 Book: Thomas' Calculus Ch.2-5 (Limits, Derivatives, Integration)
📚 NPTEL: https://nptel.ac.in (search "Engineering Mathematics" calculus)

BASIC PROBABILITY:
• P(A∪B) = P(A)+P(B)−P(A∩B)
• P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B)
• Independent: P(A∩B) = P(A)·P(B)

BAYES' THEOREM:
• P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A) / P(B)
• Total Probability: P(B) = Σ P(B|Aᵢ)·P(Aᵢ)

DISTRIBUTIONS:
• Bernoulli(p): P(1)=p, P(0)=1−p; E=p, Var=p(1−p)
• Binomial(n,p): P(k)=C(n,k)pᵏ(1−p)ⁿ⁻ᵏ; E=np, Var=np(1−p)
• Poisson(λ): P(k)=e⁻λλᵏ/k!; E=Var=λ
• Geometric(p): P(k)=(1−p)ᵏ⁻¹p; E=1/p (memoryless!)
• Exponential(λ): E=1/λ (memoryless property)
• Normal N(μ,σ²): symmetric bell curve; 68-95-99.7 rule
• Uniform[a,b]: E=(a+b)/2, Var=(b−a)²/12

STATISTICS:
• Variance = E[X²] − (E[X])²
• Std dev σ = √Var
• Covariance: Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y]
• Independent → Cov=0 (converse not always true)

GATE FOCUS: Bayes' theorem, expected value calculation, identifying distribution from description, conditional probability.

REFERENCES:
📺 Search: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=probability+bayes+theorem+gate+engineering+math
📺 Khan Academy: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=khan+academy+probability+distributions
📝 PYQs: https://gateoverflow.in/tag/probability
📚 Book: Kenneth Rosen — Discrete Math Ch.7 (Discrete Probability)
📚 NPTEL: https://nptel.ac.in (search "Probability and Statistics" IIT)

📅 June 11 – July 10, 2026

Topics:

• Sequential circuits (Flip-Flops, Counters, FSMs)

Weekly Plan:
• Week 1 (Jun 11-17): Boolean Algebra + K-Map
• Week 2 (Jun 18-24): Combinational Circuits

📅 Jun 11–17, 2026

Day 1 Thu Jun 11: Boolean Algebra — axioms, theorems, De Morgan's laws, duality principle
Day 2 Fri Jun 12: Logic Gates — AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR, XOR, XNOR; universal gates
Day 3 Sat Jun 13: Canonical forms — SOP, POS, minterms, maxterms
Day 4 Sun Jun 14: K-Map minimization — 2,3,4-variable; prime implicants, essential PI
Day 5 Mon Jun 15: K-Map with don't cares; NAND/NOR implementation
Day 6 Tue Jun 16: Quine-McCluskey method (concept level)
Day 7 Wed Jun 17: Solve 20 GATE PYQs + Revise

BOOLEAN THEOREMS:
• Identity: A+0=A, A·1=A
• Null: A+1=1, A·0=0
• Idempotent: A+A=A, A·A=A
• Complement: A+A'=1, A·A'=0
• Absorption: A+AB=A, A(A+B)=A
• De Morgan: (A+B)'=A'B', (AB)'=A'+B'

UNIVERSAL GATES:
• NAND and NOR can implement any Boolean function
• XOR: A⊕B=AB'+A'B; A⊕A=0; A⊕0=A; A⊕1=A'; associative & commutative
• XNOR: A⊙B=(A⊕B)' = AB+A'B'

CANONICAL FORMS:
• Minterm mᵢ: product term, all variables present (0→complemented, 1→uncomplemented)
• Maxterm Mᵢ: sum term, all variables present
• SOP = Σm (sum of minterms), POS = ΠM (product of maxterms)

K-MAP RULES:
• Group sizes: powers of 2 only (1,2,4,8,16)
• Groups can wrap around edges
• Don't cares (X): use to form larger groups
• Essential Prime Implicant: only PI covering a particular minterm
• Minimize: largest groups first → fewest literals

IMPLEMENTATION:
• NOT(A AND B) = NAND gate — 2-level NAND = SOP
• NOT(A OR B) = NOR gate — 2-level NOR = POS

GATE FOCUS: Minimize function using K-Map, implement using NAND gates, XOR properties, number of gates needed.

REFERENCES:
📺 Neso Academy (Digital Electronics): https://www.youtube.com/@nesoacademy
📺 Search: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=boolean+algebra+kmap+minimization+neso+academy
📝 PYQs: https://gateoverflow.in/tag/boolean-algebra

📄 PDF: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=gate+smashers+digital+logic+boolean

📅 Jun 18–24, 2026

Day 1 Thu Jun 18: Adders — Half Adder, Full Adder, Ripple Carry, Carry Look-Ahead
Day 2 Fri Jun 19: Number systems — 1's complement, 2's complement, BCD, Gray code, IEEE 754
Day 3 Sat Jun 20: Multiplexers (MUX) — 2:1, 4:1, 8:1; implementing functions with MUX
Day 4 Sun Jun 21: Demux, Decoders, Encoders, Priority Encoders
Day 5 Mon Jun 22: Comparators, Shifters, ALU design concept
Day 6 Tue Jun 23: Practice: implement Boolean functions using MUX and Decoder
Day 7 Wed Jun 24: Solve 20 GATE PYQs + Revise

ADDERS:
• Half Adder: Sum=A⊕B, Carry=AB
• Full Adder: Sum=A⊕B⊕Cin, Cout=AB+Cin(A⊕B)
• Ripple Carry: n FAs chained; delay=n×tFA (slow)
• Carry Look-Ahead (CLA): fast; computes all carries simultaneously

NUMBER REPRESENTATIONS:
• 1's complement: invert all bits
• 2's complement: invert all + 1 (most used in hardware)
• Range of n-bit 2's complement: −2^(n−1) to 2^(n−1)−1
• IEEE 754 single (32-bit): 1 sign + 8 exponent + 23 mantissa
  Exponent bias = 127; value = (−1)^s × 1.mantissa × 2^(exp−127)

MULTIPLEXER:
• 2ⁿ:1 MUX needs n select lines
• 4:1 MUX can implement any 3-variable Boolean function
• 2^(n−1):1 MUX can implement any n-variable function

DECODER:
• n-to-2ⁿ decoder; one output active at a time
• Any Boolean function = ORing selected output lines of decoder

ENCODER:
• 2ⁿ-to-n encoder; inverse of decoder
• Priority encoder: highest active input determines output

GATE FOCUS: Full adder expression, implement function using MUX, IEEE 754 conversion, 2's complement range and overflow.

REFERENCES:
📺 Neso Academy (Combinational): https://www.youtube.com/@nesoacademy
📺 Search: https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=combinational+circuits+mux+decoder+adder+gate+neso
📝 PYQs: https://gateoverflow.in/tag/combinational-circuits
📚 Book: Morris Mano — Digital Design Ch.4 (Combinational Logic)
📄 IEEE 754 Converter: https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html

📅 Jun 25 – Jul 1, 2026

Day 1 Wed Jun 25: Latches — SR, D Latch; difference between latch and flip-flop
Day 2 Thu Jun 26: Flip-Flops — SR, D, JK, T; edge triggering (positive/negative)
Day 3 Fri Jun 27: FF conversions (JK→D, T→JK etc.); excitation tables
Day 4 Sat Jun 28: Registers — shift registers (SISO, SIPO, PISO, PIPO)
Day 5 Sun Jun 29: Counters — ripple, synchronous, mod-n counter design
Day 6 Mon Jun 30: State machines — Moore vs Mealy, state diagram, state table, minimization
Day 7 Tue Jul 1: Solve 20 GATE PYQs + Revise

FLIP-FLOP TYPES:
• SR FF: S=1,R=0→Q=1; S=0,R=1→Q=0; S=R=0→unchanged; S=R=1→INVALID
• D FF: Q(next)=D (edge-triggered; most used for registers)
• JK FF: J=K=1→toggle; solves SR invalid state
• T FF: T=1→toggle; T=0→unchanged (used in counters)

EXCITATION TABLES: (what inputs needed to get desired output transition)
• D FF: D = Q(next) (trivial)
• T FF: T = Q(current) ⊕ Q(next)
• JK FF: J=1 to set, K=1 to reset, J=K=0 to hold, J=K=1 to toggle

COUNTERS:
• n flip-flops → can count up to mod-2ⁿ
• Ripple (async): each FF clocked by previous output → propagation delay
• Synchronous: all FFs share same clock → faster
• Mod-n counter: use reset/feedback logic to stop at n

FINITE STATE MACHINES:
• Moore: output depends only on current state
• Mealy: output depends on state AND input (fewer states than Moore)
• State minimization: merge equivalent states (same future behavior)
• DFA (from TOC) is a Moore machine

GATE FOCUS: JK/D flip-flop state trace, convert between FF types using excitation table, design mod-n counter, Moore vs Mealy distinction.

REFERENCES:
📺 Neso Academy (Sequential): https://www.youtube.com/@nesoacademy

📝 PYQs: https://gateoverflow.in/tag/sequential-circuits
📚 Book: Morris Mano — Digital Design Ch.5-6 (Synchronous Sequential Logic)

📅 Jul 2–10, 2026

Day 1 Thu Jul 2: ISA — instruction formats, addressing modes, instruction types
Day 2 Fri Jul 3: ALU, data path, control unit (hardwired vs microprogrammed)
Day 3 Sat Jul 4: Memory hierarchy — registers, cache, RAM, disk; locality of reference
Day 4 Sun Jul 5: Cache — direct mapped, set associative, fully associative; hit/miss
Day 5 Mon Jul 6: Cache — replacement policies (LRU, FIFO, OPT), write policies

Day 8-9 Jul 9-10: Solve 30 PYQs + Month 2 full revision test

ADDRESSING MODES:
• Immediate: operand in instruction itself
• Direct: instruction contains memory address
• Indirect: instruction contains address of address
• Register: operand in register
• Register Indirect: register contains address
• Displacement/Base+Offset: EA = Base + Offset
• PC-Relative: EA = PC + Offset (for branches)

CACHE MEMORY:
• Hit ratio h → avg access time = h×Tc + (1−h)×(Tc+Tm)
• Direct Mapped: block mod (# cache lines) → simple, low cost, high conflict
• k-way Set Associative: block maps to set of k lines
• Fully Associative: any block anywhere → max flexibility, complex LRU
• Write-through: write to both cache and memory simultaneously
• Write-back: write to cache, update memory when evicted (dirty bit)

PIPELINING:
• k-stage pipeline speedup for n instructions: (n×k)/(n+k−1) → approaches k for large n
• CPI = 1 + stall cycles per instruction
• Hazards:
  - Data Hazard (RAW, WAR, WAW): forwarding/bypassing reduces stalls
  - Control Hazard: branches → branch prediction or delayed branching
  - Structural Hazard: two instructions need same hardware

I/O:
• Polling: CPU repeatedly checks device status (wastes CPU cycles)
• Interrupt-driven: device interrupts CPU when ready (efficient)
• DMA: device transfers data directly to memory; CPU not involved

GATE FOCUS: Average memory access time with cache, pipeline speedup formula, stall cycle calculation, addressing mode effective address.

BINARY TREE PROPERTIES:
• Max nodes at level i (root=0): 2^i
• Max nodes in tree of height h: 2^(h+1)−1
• Min height of n-node complete BT: ⌊log₂n⌋
• Inorder traversal of BST gives sorted sequence!

TRAVERSALS:
• Inorder: Left→Root→Right (sorted for BST)
• Preorder: Root→Left→Right (copy/serialize tree)
• Postorder: Left→Right→Root (delete tree)
• Given pre+in OR post+in: can uniquely reconstruct tree
  (Need both in+pre or in+post; pre+post alone not unique)

BST OPERATIONS (all O(h)):
• Search: compare with root, recurse left or right
• Insert: search for position, add leaf
• Delete: (1) leaf→remove; (2) one child→replace; (3) two children→replace with inorder successor
• Skewed BST: h=n−1 → O(n); Balanced BST: h=O(log n)

AVL TREE:
• Balance factor = height(left)−height(right) ∈ {−1, 0, 1}
• Rotations:
  LL: right rotation at unbalanced node
  RR: left rotation at unbalanced node
  LR: left rotation on left child, then right rotation on node
  RL: right rotation on right child, then left rotation on node
• Height of AVL with n nodes: O(log n) guaranteed

HEAP:
• Complete binary tree stored in array; index from 1: parent=⌊i/2⌋, left=2i, right=2i+1
• Max-heap: parent≥children; Min-heap: parent≤children
• Heapify (sift-down): O(log n); Build heap from array: O(n)
• Insert: add at end, sift-up O(log n); Extract-max: swap root with last, sift-down O(log n)

GATE FOCUS: Tree traversal output, reconstruct tree, AVL rotation type, heap operation result, BST delete.

GRAPH REPRESENTATIONS:
• Adjacency Matrix: O(V²) space, O(1) edge query, O(V) to list neighbors
• Adjacency List: O(V+E) space, O(degree) edge query — preferred for sparse graphs

BFS (uses Queue):
• O(V+E) time; O(V) space
• Finds shortest path in unweighted graphs
• Tree edges + cross edges in BFS tree

DFS (uses Stack/Recursion):
• O(V+E) time
• Tree edges + back edges (cycle!) + forward edges + cross edges
• Back edge in undirected DFS → cycle exists
• All edges in undirected DFS are tree or back edges

TOPOLOGICAL SORT (only for DAG):
• DFS-based: push to stack after visiting all descendants; reverse order
• Kahn's: start with in-degree 0 nodes, BFS; if all nodes processed → no cycle

HASHING:
• Load factor α = n/m (n=items, m=table size)
• Chaining: avg search O(1+α); allows α>1
• Open Addressing: all elements in table; requires α<1
  Linear Probing: h(k,i)=(h(k)+i) mod m → primary clustering
  Quadratic Probing: h(k,i)=(h(k)+c₁i+c₂i²) mod m → secondary clustering
  Double Hashing: h(k,i)=(h₁(k)+i·h₂(k)) mod m → best, minimal clustering
• Successful search expected probes (linear): ½(1+1/(1−α))
• Unsuccessful search expected probes (linear): ½(1+(1/(1−α))²)

ASYMPTOTIC NOTATION:
• O(g): upper bound — f(n)=O(g) if f≤c×g for large n
• Ω(g): lower bound — f(n)=Ω(g) if f≥c×g for large n
• Θ(g): tight bound — both O and Ω
• Order: O(1)<O(log n)<O(√n)<O(n)<O(n log n)<O(n²)<O(n³)<O(2ⁿ)<O(n!)

MASTER THEOREM for T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n):
• Case 1: f(n)=O(n^(log_b(a)−ε)) → T(n)=Θ(n^log_b(a))
• Case 2: f(n)=Θ(n^log_b(a)) → T(n)=Θ(n^log_b(a) × log n)
• Case 3: f(n)=Ω(n^(log_b(a)+ε)) and regularity → T(n)=Θ(f(n))

SORTING SUMMARY:
Algorithm  | Best      | Avg       | Worst     | Space  | Stable
Bubble     | O(n)      | O(n²)     | O(n²)     | O(1)   | Yes
Selection  | O(n²)     | O(n²)     | O(n²)     | O(1)   | No
Insertion  | O(n)      | O(n²)     | O(n²)     | O(1)   | Yes
Merge      | O(n log n)| O(n log n)| O(n log n)| O(n)   | Yes
Quick      | O(n log n)| O(n log n)| O(n²)     |O(log n)| No
Heap       | O(n log n)| O(n log n)| O(n log n)| O(1)   | No
Counting   | O(n+k)    | O(n+k)    | O(n+k)    | O(k)   | Yes
Radix      | O(nk)     | O(nk)     | O(nk)     | O(n+k) | Yes

KEY NOTES:
• Quick sort worst case: sorted input with pivot=first element
• Merge sort: guaranteed O(n log n) regardless of input
• Counting/Radix/Bucket: linear but require constraints on data
• Lower bound for comparison-based sorting: Ω(n log n)

SINGLE SOURCE SHORTEST PATH:
• Dijkstra: O((V+E) log V) with min-heap; does NOT work with negative edges
  Algorithm: relaxation — if d[v]+w(v,u) < d[u] then update d[u]
• Bellman-Ford: O(VE); relax all edges V−1 times; detect negative cycle if more relaxation possible
• BFS: O(V+E) for unweighted graphs (all edge weights = 1)

ALL-PAIRS SHORTEST PATH:
• Floyd-Warshall: O(V³); DP approach
  d[i][j] = min(d[i][j], d[i][k]+d[k][j]) for each intermediate k
  Works with negative edges; detects negative cycle if d[i][i]<0

MINIMUM SPANNING TREE:
• Greedy property: adding cheapest edge that doesn't create a cycle
• Kruskal: Sort edges by weight; add if no cycle (use Union-Find); O(E log E)
• Prim: Start from any vertex; always add min-weight edge to MST; O((V+E) log V)
• Both give same MST WEIGHT; structure may differ
• MST is unique if all edge weights are distinct
• MST has V−1 edges for a V-vertex graph
• Cut property: minimum edge crossing any cut belongs to MST

UNION-FIND (for Kruskal):
• find(x): return root with path compression
• union(x,y): union by rank (smaller tree attached to larger)
• Amortized O(α(n)) per operation

DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING:
Requires: Optimal Substructure + Overlapping Subproblems

KEY PROBLEMS:
• 0/1 Knapsack: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i−1][w], dp[i−1][w−wᵢ]+vᵢ); O(nW)
• LCS(X,Y): dp[i][j] = dp[i−1][j−1]+1 if X[i]=Y[j] else max(dp[i−1][j], dp[i][j−1]); O(mn)
• LIS: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) for all j<i where A[j]<A[i]; O(n²) or O(n log n) with patience sort
• Edit Distance: dp[i][j] = dp[i−1][j−1] if same; else 1+min(dp[i−1][j], dp[i][j−1], dp[i−1][j−1])
• Matrix Chain: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j]+pᵢ₋₁·pₖ·pⱼ) for i≤k<j; O(n³)
• Coin Change (min coins): dp[i] = min(dp[i−coin]+1) for each coin; O(amount×coins)

GREEDY ALGORITHMS:
Works when local optimal → global optimal (not always provable, check carefully)

• Activity Selection: sort by finish time, greedily pick non-overlapping; O(n log n)
• Fractional Knapsack: sort by value/weight ratio, fill greedily; O(n log n)
• Huffman Coding: build prefix-free code using min-heap of frequencies; O(n log n)
  Path length = Σ(frequency × depth); Greedy builds optimal prefix-free code

DP vs GREEDY:
• 0/1 Knapsack: MUST use DP (greedy fails)
• Fractional Knapsack: Greedy works
• Coin Change (arbitrary): DP; US coin denominations: greedy works

CHOMSKY HIERARCHY:
Regular (Type 3) ⊂ CFL (Type 2) ⊂ CSL (Type 1) ⊂ RE (Type 0)
DFA/NFA/RegEx → Regular | PDA → CFL | LBA → CSL | TM → RE

DFA/NFA:
• DFA: deterministic, δ: Q×Σ→Q (exactly one transition)
• NFA: δ: Q×Σ→2^Q (subset of states, may have ε-transitions)
• Every NFA has equivalent DFA (subset construction; at most 2^n states)
• DFA minimization: merge states that are indistinguishable
  Two states indistinguishable if same future behavior for all inputs

REGULAR LANGUAGES:
Closed under: union ✓, intersection ✓, complement ✓, concatenation ✓, Kleene star ✓, reversal ✓
NOT closed under: nothing (RL is closed under all Boolean operations)

Pumping Lemma (RL): ∀ regular L, ∃p: any w∈L with |w|≥p can be split w=xyz
where |xy|≤p, |y|≥1, and xyⁱz ∈ L for all i≥0
Use to PROVE language is NOT regular (show some string violates this)

NOT regular: {aⁿbⁿ:n≥0}, {ww:w∈Σ*}, {w:|w| is prime}

CONTEXT-FREE LANGUAGES:
Closed under: union ✓, concatenation ✓, Kleene star ✓
NOT closed under: intersection ✗, complement ✗
Exception: CFL ∩ Regular = CFL ✓

Ambiguous grammar: multiple leftmost (or rightmost) derivations for same string
Inherent ambiguity: every grammar for the language is ambiguous

TURING MACHINE:
• Decidable (recursive): TM always halts (accepts or rejects)
• Recognizable (recursively enumerable): TM halts and accepts if w∈L; may loop if w∉L
• Halting problem: UNDECIDABLE (canonical undecidable problem)
• Rice's Theorem: any non-trivial property of TM languages is undecidable

• FCFS: non-preemptive, simple; convoy effect (short jobs wait for long jobs)
• SJF (non-preemptive): picks shortest burst time job; optimal average waiting time for non-preemptive
• SRTF (preemptive SJF): optimal average waiting time overall; needs future burst time
• Priority: can starve low-priority processes; fix with aging (gradually increase priority)
• Round Robin: quantum q; good response time; avg WT depends on q
  If q→∞: behaves like FCFS; if q→0: behaves like SJF (impractical)

SCHEDULING METRICS:
• Turnaround Time (TAT) = Completion Time − Arrival Time
• Waiting Time (WT) = TAT − Burst Time
• Response Time = First CPU allocation − Arrival Time

CRITICAL SECTION — 3 REQUIREMENTS:
1. Mutual Exclusion: only one process in CS
2. Progress: if no process in CS, decision on entry must be made in finite time
3. Bounded Waiting: finite bound on number of times others can enter before you

SEMAPHORES:
• wait(S): S−−; if S<0, block the process
• signal(S): S++; if S≤0, wake up a blocked process
• Binary semaphore = mutex (0 or 1 only)
• Counting semaphore: allows multiple concurrent access

CLASSIC PROBLEMS:
• Producer-Consumer: full semaphore, empty semaphore, mutex
• Readers-Writers: multiple readers OK, exclusive writers
• Dining Philosophers: 5 philosophers, 5 forks — avoid deadlock!

GATE FOCUS: Draw Gantt chart, compute avg waiting/turnaround time for given scheduling algorithm, semaphore usage trace.

DEADLOCK CONDITIONS (all 4 must hold simultaneously):
1. Mutual Exclusion: resource held exclusively
2. Hold and Wait: process holds ≥1 resource while waiting for another
3. No Preemption: resources not forcibly taken
4. Circular Wait: chain P1→R1→P2→R2→...→P1

BANKER'S ALGORITHM:
• Safety Algorithm: find process where Need≤Available; simulate it finishing, reclaim resources, repeat
• If all processes can finish → SAFE STATE
• Resource Request: if Request≤Need and Request≤Available: tentatively grant, check safety
  If safe → grant permanently; else rollback

PAGING:
• Logical address = page number (p) + offset (d)
• Physical address = frame number (f) + offset (d); same offset!
• TLB hit: access time = TLB time + 1 memory access
• TLB miss: access time = TLB time + 2 memory accesses (page table + data)
• EAT = h×(Tm+Tc) + (1−h)×(2Tm+Tc) where h=hit ratio, Tc=TLB time, Tm=memory time

PAGE REPLACEMENT:
• FIFO: replace oldest page; simple; subject to Belady's anomaly (more frames→more faults!)
• OPT: replace page used farthest in future; theoretical best; not practical
• LRU: replace least recently used; no Belady's anomaly; better than FIFO
• LRU approximation: reference bit (clock algorithm)

FILE ALLOCATION:
• Contiguous: fast sequential access, external fragmentation, hard to grow
• Linked: no fragmentation, poor random access, no direct access
• Indexed: index block holds all pointers; good random access, overhead for small files
• UNIX inode: direct(12) + single indirect + double indirect + triple indirect blocks

GATE FOCUS: Banker's algorithm safe sequence, page replacement fault count with reference string, EAT with TLB hit ratio.

OSI vs TCP/IP:
• OSI (7 layers): Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
• TCP/IP (4 layers): Link, Internet, Transport, Application
• TCP/IP is the practical model; OSI is conceptual reference

APPLICATION LAYER PROTOCOLS:
• HTTP: TCP port 80; request-response; stateless; HTTP/2 multiplexed
• HTTPS: HTTP + TLS; port 443
• FTP: TCP port 21 (control) + 20 (data); active vs passive mode
• SMTP: TCP port 25 (sending email); POP3 port 110; IMAP port 143

  Recursive query: resolver does all work; Iterative: each server gives next referral
• DHCP: UDP; assigns IP dynamically; DORA (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge)

TCP 3-WAY HANDSHAKE:
• Client→Server: SYN (seq=x)
• Server→Client: SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)
• Client→Server: ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1)
Connection established after 3 messages (1.5 RTT delay)

TCP FLOW CONTROL:
• Receive window (rwnd): receiver advertises free buffer space
• Sender can have at most rwnd bytes unacknowledged in flight
• Throughput ≈ Window Size / RTT

TCP CONGESTION CONTROL:
• Slow Start: cwnd starts at 1 MSS, doubles each RTT until ssthresh
• Congestion Avoidance: cwnd increases by 1 MSS per RTT
• Timeout: ssthresh=cwnd/2, cwnd=1 (restart slow start)
• 3 duplicate ACKs: ssthresh=cwnd/2, cwnd=ssthresh (fast recovery)

GATE FOCUS: TCP window size and throughput calculation, DNS resolution steps, TCP handshake states, congestion control cwnd trace.

IPv4 SUBNETTING:
• /n notation: first n bits are network, rest are host
• /24 = 256 addresses, 254 usable (subtract network and broadcast)
• Subnet formula: 2^h − 2 usable hosts (h = host bits)
• Broadcast = set all host bits to 1
• Example: 192.168.1.0/26 → 64 addresses, 62 hosts, next subnet starts at .64

ROUTING PROTOCOLS:
• Distance Vector (RIP): share distance table with neighbors; Bellman-Ford; slow convergence; count-to-infinity problem; max 15 hops
• Link State (OSPF): flood topology to all routers; each runs Dijkstra; fast convergence; scalable
• BGP: path vector; between Autonomous Systems; policy-based routing

ERROR DETECTION:
• Parity: single parity detects odd errors; 2D parity corrects 1 error
• Checksum: add words, complement; used in IP/TCP/UDP headers
• CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check): polynomial division; detects all burst errors ≤ r bits (r-bit CRC)
  Sender appends r-bit remainder; receiver checks remainder = 0
• Hamming Code: corrects 1 error, detects 2 errors; distance-3 code

MAC PROTOCOLS:
• CSMA/CD (Ethernet 802.3): sense → transmit → detect collision → stop → backoff (exponential)
  Minimum frame size: 2×propagation delay × bandwidth
• CSMA/CA (WiFi 802.11): RTS/CTS before transmit; no collision detection in wireless
• Pure ALOHA: S=Ge^(−2G), max efficiency 1/2e ≈ 18.4%
• Slotted ALOHA: S=Ge^(−G), max efficiency 1/e ≈ 36.8%

NETWORK DEVICES:
• Hub: Layer 1, repeater, broadcasts to all ports
• Switch: Layer 2, learns MAC table, forwards to specific port
• Router: Layer 3, routes based on IP address, connects networks

GATE FOCUS: Subnet calculation (hosts, subnets, addresses), CRC remainder, ALOHA efficiency, routing protocol comparison.

text: DBMS — ER Model, Relational Algebra & SQL
description:
ER MODEL:
• Entity: real-world object; Strong entity has own key; Weak entity depends on owner
• Attributes: Simple (atomic), Composite (has sub-parts), Multivalued {age}, Derived (age from DOB)
• Cardinality: 1:1, 1:N (one-to-many), M:N (many-to-many)
• Participation: Total (every entity participates — double line) vs Partial (some participate — single line)
• Weak entity: uses partial key + owner's key together as primary key

ER TO RELATIONAL MAPPING:
• Strong entity → relation with same attributes
• M:N relationship → separate relation with PKs of both entities
• 1:N relationship → add FK to the N-side entity
• Multivalued attribute → separate relation
• Weak entity → relation including owner's PK + partial key

RELATIONAL ALGEBRA:
• σ_condition(R): select tuples (rows) satisfying condition
• π_attrs(R): project (select columns; removes duplicate tuples)
• R ⋈ S: natural join on common attributes
• R ⋈_c S: theta join with condition c
• Left Outer Join (⟕): all left tuples + matching right; unmatched right = NULL
• Division R÷S: tuples in R's projection associated with ALL tuples in S

SQL ESSENTIALS:
• SELECT...FROM...WHERE...GROUP BY...HAVING...ORDER BY
• NULL: use IS NULL not = NULL; COUNT(*) counts NULLs, COUNT(col) ignores them
• Aggregate: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN — applied after GROUP BY
• HAVING filters groups; WHERE filters rows before grouping
• Correlated subquery: inner query references outer query's column

GATE FOCUS: Write RA expression for English query, translate SQL to RA, identify ER cardinality, construct join results.

FUNCTIONAL DEPENDENCIES:
• Armstrong's Axioms: Reflexivity (Y⊆X → X→Y), Augmentation (X→Y → XZ→YZ), Transitivity (X→Y,Y→Z → X→Z)
• Closure X⁺: all attributes determined by X using given FDs
• Candidate Key: minimal set where X⁺ = all attributes
• Prime attribute: part of some candidate key

NORMAL FORMS:
• 1NF: all attribute values are atomic (no repeating groups, no multivalued attributes)
• 2NF: 1NF + no PARTIAL dependency of non-prime attribute on any candidate key
  (Only an issue if CK has multiple attributes)
• 3NF: 2NF + no TRANSITIVE dependency (A→B→C where B is not a superkey)
  3NF can preserve all FDs; allows some redundancy
• BCNF: for every FD X→Y, X must be a superkey
  Stronger than 3NF; may not preserve all FDs

SERL TEST FOR 3NF: A→B is allowed if: A is superkey OR B is prime attribute

TRANSACTION ACID:
• Atomicity: all operations or none
• Consistency: DB valid state before and after
• Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere
• Durability: committed transactions persist

SERIALIZABILITY:
• Conflict: two operations access same data, at least one is write, from different transactions
• Conflict serializable iff precedence graph has NO CYCLE
• Precedence graph: Tᵢ→Tⱼ if Tᵢ's operation conflicts with and precedes Tⱼ's

TWO-PHASE LOCKING (2PL):
• Phase 1 (Growing): acquire locks, no releasing
• Phase 2 (Shrinking): release locks, no acquiring
• 2PL guarantees conflict serializability
• Strict 2PL: hold all locks until commit/abort (prevents cascading rollback)

GATE FOCUS: Find candidate keys using FD closure, identify BCNF violation, check conflict serializability using precedence graph.

COMPILER PHASES:

LEXICAL ANALYSIS:
• Token: <category, value> e.g., <ID, "x">, <NUM, 42>, <KEYWORD, "if">
• Lexeme: actual string; Pattern: RE describing lexeme
• NFA from RE (Thompson's) → DFA (subset construction) → Minimized DFA

FIRST AND FOLLOW SETS:
• FIRST(α): terminals that can begin a string derived from α
  If α can derive ε, include ε in FIRST(α)
• FOLLOW(A): terminals that can appear immediately to the right of A
  Add $ to FOLLOW(start symbol)
  If A→αBβ: FIRST(β)−{ε} ⊆ FOLLOW(B); if ε∈FIRST(β): FOLLOW(A)⊆FOLLOW(B)

LL(1) PARSING:
• Parsing table M[A,a]: add production A→α to M[A,a] for a∈FIRST(α)
  If ε∈FIRST(α): add to M[A,b] for all b∈FOLLOW(A)
• LL(1) grammar: no conflicts in parsing table (max one production per cell)
• Must eliminate: left recursion (A→Aα) and left factoring (common prefix)

LR PARSING POWER: LR(0) < SLR(1) < LALR(1) < LR(1)
• LR(0) items: productions with a dot showing parsing position
• SLR: use FOLLOW sets to resolve conflicts
• LALR(1): merge LR(1) states with same core; used in yacc/bison
• Shift-Reduce conflict, Reduce-Reduce conflict

INTERMEDIATE CODE (3-address code):
• x = y op z; x = op y; x = y; goto L; if x rel y goto L; param x; call p,n; return y

GATE FOCUS: Compute FIRST/FOLLOW sets, build LL(1) parse table, identify LR(0) items, recognize conflicts.

SDLC MODELS:
• Waterfall: sequential phases; rigid; best for well-understood stable requirements
• V-Model: testing phase mirrors each development phase; validation + verification
• Agile: iterative sprints; flexible; customer collaboration; best for changing requirements
• Spiral: risk-driven; iterative; for large, high-risk projects

SOFTWARE DESIGN:
Coupling (between modules — LOWER IS BETTER):
Content < Common < Control < Stamp < Data
(Data coupling = best — only pass necessary data)

Cohesion (within module — HIGHER IS BETTER):
Coincidental < Logical < Temporal < Procedural < Communicational < Sequential < Functional
(Functional cohesion = best — module does one well-defined task)

TESTING:
• White-box (structural): needs source code
  - Statement coverage: execute every statement
  - Branch coverage: execute every branch (both true/false); stronger than statement
  - Path coverage: execute every path; strongest; may be infinite with loops
• Black-box (functional): no source code needed
  - Equivalence Partitioning: group inputs into equivalence classes
  - Boundary Value Analysis: test at boundaries of valid range
  - Decision table testing

CYCLOMATIC COMPLEXITY (McCabe):
• CC = E − N + 2P (E=edges, N=nodes, P=connected components)
• For single function: CC = binary decisions + 1
• CC = number of independent paths in the program
• CC ≤ 10: well-structured; CC > 10: complex, needs refactoring

CRITICAL PATH METHOD:
• Draw activity network; find longest path (critical path)
• Float/Slack = Latest Start − Earliest Start = 0 for critical activities
• Total project duration = length of critical path

GATE FOCUS: Calculate cyclomatic complexity, identify coupling/cohesion type, test coverage comparison, critical path in network.

ENGLISH GRAMMAR — Key Rules:

TENSES:
• Simple Present: He runs / She writes
• Present Perfect: He has run / She has written (action with present relevance)
• Past Perfect: He had run before... (earlier of two past actions)
• Rule: with 'since' → Present Perfect; with 'ago' → Simple Past

ARTICLES:
• 'a' before consonant sound: a university, a European
• 'an' before vowel sound: an hour, an MBA
• 'the' for specific/known nouns; no article for general plural or uncountable

SUBJECT-VERB AGREEMENT:
• Collective nouns (committee, team) → singular verb
• Either/Neither → singular verb
• Each/Every → singular verb
• Neither...nor / Either...or → verb agrees with nearest subject

PREPOSITIONS:
• Depends on: agree WITH, differ FROM, conform TO, result IN, rely ON

VOICE (Active → Passive):
• Active: She writes a letter.
• Passive: A letter is written by her.
• Rule: Object becomes Subject; verb → be + past participle; subject → by + agent

VOCABULARY TIPS:
• Learn word roots: port (carry), dict (say), aud (hear), vis (see)
• Practice antonyms/synonyms in context, not in isolation
• Common confusables: affect/effect, principle/principal, compliment/complement

READING COMPREHENSION STRATEGY:
• Read questions FIRST, then the passage
• Look for keywords from questions in passage
• Main idea = usually first or last sentence of paragraph
• Inference = what MUST be true, not just possibly true

NUMBER THEORY:
• HCF × LCM = Product of two numbers
• Divisibility rules: by 3 (digit sum divisible by 3), by 4 (last 2 digits), by 9 (digit sum by 9), by 11 (alternating sum)
• Remainder theorem: if f(x) divided by (x-a), remainder = f(a)
• Fermat's little theorem: a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p) for prime p, gcd(a,p)=1

PERCENTAGE & PROFIT-LOSS:
• % change = (Change/Original) × 100
• If price ↑ by r%, to restore: reduce by r/(100+r) × 100
• Profit% = (Profit/CP) × 100; SP = CP × (100+P%)/100
• Successive discounts of a% and b%: net discount = a+b − ab/100

RATIO, PROPORTION & MIXTURES:
• Mixture rule (Alligation): (C2−Cavg):(Cavg−C1) gives ratio to mix
• Partnership: profit shared in ratio of capital × time

TIME, SPEED, DISTANCE:
• Speed = Distance/Time
• Relative speed: same direction = |S1−S2|; opposite direction = S1+S2
• Boats: upstream = B−R, downstream = B+R
• Time = Distance/Speed; Average speed = 2S1S2/(S1+S2) for equal distances

TIME & WORK:
• If A does work in x days, rate = 1/x
• A+B together: rate = 1/x + 1/y; time = xy/(x+y)
• Pipes: filling pipe adds, leaking pipe subtracts

SIMPLE & COMPOUND INTEREST:
• SI = PRT/100
• CI = P(1 + R/100)^T − P
• CI−SI for 2 years = P(R/100)²

NUMBER & LETTER SERIES:
• Arithmetic series: constant difference (2,5,8,11...)
• Geometric series: constant ratio (3,6,12,24...)
• Difference series: differences form another series
• Prime/square/cube number series
• Letter series: follow alphabetical positions (A=1, B=2,..., Z=26)
• Mixed series: combine two interleaved series
• Tip: always check differences, then differences of differences

ANALOGY:
• Tool → Function: Pen:Write :: Knife:Cut
• Part → Whole: Page:Book :: Petal:Flower
• Cause → Effect: Fire:Smoke :: Rain:Flood
• Find the RELATIONSHIP, then apply to answer pair

BLOOD RELATIONS:
• Draw a family tree; use standardized notation
• Key terms: maternal (mother's side), paternal (father's side)
• Siblings share both parents; half-siblings share one parent

DIRECTION & DISTANCE:
• Always face North initially
• Right turn = clockwise, Left turn = anticlockwise
• Draw path on paper; use Pythagorean theorem for final distance

DATA INTERPRETATION:
• Bar Chart: compare heights of bars
• Pie Chart: % of total; sector angle = (%/100) × 360°
• Line Graph: trends over time
• Table: direct reading; calculate % change, ratio, average
• Tip: approximate — don't calculate exact values under time pressure

SEATING ARRANGEMENT / PUZZLES:
• Circular arrangement: fix one person, arrange remaining (n−1)!
• Linear arrangement: n! arrangements
• Always write down constraints before solving

📅 30 MINUTES DAILY — Every single day through Month 1 to Month 7.

MONTH 1-2 (May–Jul): Foundation
• 15 min Verbal: grammar rules + 5 vocab questions
• 15 min Numerical: basic arithmetic (%, ratio, TSD, work)
• Target: clear all formula-based concepts

MONTH 3-4 (Jul–Sep): Speed Building
• 10 min Verbal: RC passage + sentence correction
• 20 min Numerical: mix of all topics; target accuracy > 80%
• Start timed practice: 5 questions in 6 minutes

MONTH 5-6 (Sep–Nov): GATE-Level Practice
• Solve only GATE PYQ aptitude questions
• Target: 10 questions in 12 minutes
• Focus: eliminate wrong answer choices, not just find right answer

MONTH 7 (Nov–Dec): Polish
• Do 15 GA questions every alternate day under timed conditions
• Aim for 13-14 out of 15 GA marks consistently

QUICK STRATEGIES FOR GATE GA:
• Reading Comprehension: 3-4 questions from 1 passage — spend max 4 min total
• Numerical: if calculation is complex, eliminate options using approximation
• Sentence ordering: look for pronouns (it/they/this) to link sentences
• Series: check last 3 terms first to identify pattern quickly
